Fees

Meridian does not publish fixed rates. Dispute resolution does not lend itself to standardized pricing.

The scope of a mediation engagement depends on the complexity of the dispute, the number of parties involved, the volume of preparation required, and the number of sessions needed to reach resolution. A straightforward two-party commercial dispute and a multi-beneficiary estate conflict are fundamentally different in scope — and pricing that pretends otherwise would be misleading.

What to Expect

Fees are discussed transparently and agreed upon during the initial consultation, before any commitment is made to proceed. You will know exactly what the engagement involves — and what it will cost — before you decide whether to move forward.

There is no fee for the initial consultation. It exists to assess your situation, determine whether mediation is appropriate, and give you the information you need to make that decision.

Cost Relative to Litigation

Mediation is, in most cases, significantly less expensive than litigation or arbitration — and faster. The goal is not simply to be cheaper, but to resolve disputes more efficiently and with outcomes that the parties themselves have shaped. Those outcomes tend to be more durable than imposed decisions.

How Fees Are Structured

Fees are typically structured on a session or engagement basis, depending on the matter. Where parties share the cost of mediation equally — which is common — that arrangement is confirmed in writing at the outset.

Discuss fees during a free consultation

No rates are committed to before you have a clear picture of the process and what your matter involves.

Book a Free Consultation

Meridian Dispute Resolution Inc

Mediation services across Canada. Based in Saskatchewan. Not legal counsel. Not therapy.
